[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2023
[TITLE]: Tess Larquemin
[METADATA/INTRO]: Hello¶ Hi there, I’m Tess. Welcome to my documentation website for Fabricademy.¶...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: During my dance cursus I discovered many amazing choreographer like Pina Bausch, who worked on transcendance through a ballet called «Le sacre du printemps». This ballet show us exhausted dancers, leading them to the limit of their strength until playing with death. To spotlights the exhaustion of dancers, Pina Bausch used nude stretch dresses which show us easily the sweating and the thinness of dancers. Through the costume, she again spotlight the vulnerability of dancers. This show completely fascinated me, that’s why I decided to participate one year after at a reproduction of one part of this ballet with the conservatory.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: TANZTHEATER WUPPERTAL PINA BAUSCH - Solomons Says
[METADATA/INTRO]: TANZTHEATER WUPPERTAL PINA BAUSCH Pina Bausch died suddenly in 2009 within a few days of a diagnosis of cancer, but her company lives on under the direction of Adolphe Binder and managing director Dirk Hesse. In its sold out, two-week...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: The women inTHE RITE OF SPRING by Pina Bausch 
 Bausch’s “The Rite of Spring” (1975) cleaves closely to Stravinsky’s rhythmically intricate score and scenario of a maiden being chosen to sacrifice herself by dancing herself to death. The cast of thirty dancers already gives the piece strong visual impact. The women are in tan, gossamer, midi-length slips, the bare-chested men wear black trousers, and they dance on a floor of rust-colored dirt. Sets and costumes for both ballets are by Rolf Borzik, who understands the power of simplicity. 
 The women inTHE RITE OF SPRING by Pina Bausch 
 The choreography sets up a strict segregation between genders — women enter first, then men. They often pack tightly together, moving as a unit, bursting into violent spasms as the music dictates. The dancing is primal and rough, although a few instances of dancerly leg extensions or modern dance-like presentation sneak in — especially from the youngest dancers. For the general audience, however, such lapses in grittiness are likely imperceptible and overall don’t detract from the savage urgency of the work.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2017
[TITLE]: REVIEW: Tanztheater Wuppertal Pina Bausch at BAM — Stage Left
[METADATA/INTRO]: REVIEW: Tanztheater Wuppertal Pina Bausch at BAM Pina Bausch, one of the most influential and original choreographers of the 20th century, once famously said: “I’m not interested in how people move but what moves them.” Her two most iconic works,...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: For “The Rite of Spring”, a more traditional modern dance piece set to Igor Stravinsky’s monumental composition, the stage is cleared and covered in a thick layer of rich, brown dirt, providing a literal earthen grounding for the violent, ferocious, and primal ritual that ensues. This piece, performed by the entire company, is perhaps the most compelling and moving dance I have ever seen. The precision of the ensemble, coupled with the emotional intensity coursing through their bodies, is at once frightening and spectacular. As they work up a sweat, their breathing echoes through the space, and the dirt begins to stick to their sinewy forms. The climax, a full-bodied, writhing solo performed by Tsai-Chin Yu teems with abandon that feels dangerous and wholly expressive.
